relevance_reasoning: Primary dataset is microsatellite genotypes (genetic_analysis) for wood turtles sampled in 2006–2007 (two years) with spatial extent not reported. Presence of an IUCN-listed endangered species increases relevance, yielding a moderate (M) relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The authors provide a primary capture dataset of raccoons and striped skunks (abundance data) in Montérégie and Estrie, Québec (data type = high relevance). However, the abstract does not report temporal extent or spatial area covered, so temporal and spatial criteria are unknown, yielding an overall low relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Primary dataset comprises annual home-range records (individual-year rows) from VHF (1999–2000) and GPS (2004–2011) telemetry on 87 caribou, so data_type = distribution. Temporal coverage is 1999–2011 (≥12 years = high) but spatial extent (km2) is not stated; applying the relevance rules yields an overall moderate (M) relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The dataset contains multi-year (2004–2011) repeatability data on habitat selection for woodland caribou in Québec (moderate temporal extent). The primary data type is habitat-selection metrics (categorized here as 'other'), yielding a conservative baseline relevance of low, but the score is upgraded because the study concerns a threatened species, resulting in a moderate (M) relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Primary data are microsatellite genotypes (genetic_analysis = high). Temporal coverage is 2011–2014 (4 years = moderate). Spatial extent is not quantified in km² (spatial extent unknown), and the unknown spatial extent lowers overall applicability for Quebec monitoring, yielding an overall relevance of L.

Primary dataset consists of microsatellite genotypes (18 loci) for 23 Acer saccharum populations (genetic_analysis = high-value data type). However, the abstract provides no explicit temporal extent or quantitative spatial range, so the overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring is downgraded to low.

Primary data are autosomal microsatellite genotypes (genetic_analysis) from 98 Canis individuals collected 2010–2014 (genetic data = high). Temporal coverage is 5 years (moderate) but spatial extent is not quantified in the text (unknown), which lowers the overall score; the special-case rule would reduce relevance, but explicit inclusion of a threatened species (Eastern Wolf) and reported range extensions upgrades the relevance to moderate for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The authors used plot-based data reporting species relative proportions/abundance (data type = abundance) over a very large spatial extent (535,355 km2), but no temporal extent is reported. Because temporal extent is not stated (which triggers a major reduction for monitoring relevance) the overall relevance is scored as low.

Primary data are GPS location records (presence-only) of migratory caribou (2007–2014) and MODIS-derived ice/water time series (2000–2014) with historical observations back to 1947, giving a long temporal extent but no spatial extent in km2 reported. The dataset is located in northern Québec (Rivière-aux-Feuilles herd), which increases relevance for northern Quebec monitoring, yielding an overall moderate relevance.

Data are molecular genotypes (genetic_analysis = high relevance) collected across four years (2002–2005, moderate temporal extent) but from a single small river (limited spatial scope), so overall relevance for Quebec monitoring is moderate.

The authors provide primary datasets including SNP genotype files and multiple trait/fitness measurements (genetic_analysis and traits), which are relevant for Quebec biodiversity monitoring; however, temporal and spatial extent are not reported in the text, so overall relevance is rated low.

Primary data are abundance-based trap captures of small mammals and beetles (high-value data type), sampled in 2007 and 2011 (moderate temporal extent) but the spatial extent in km2 is not reported. Because spatial extent is not stated and reduces applicability for Quebec-wide monitoring, overall relevance is set to Low.

Primary data are individual-level trait measurements (body mass) and breeding success collected over 2005–2011 (7 years), but spatial extent is not stated; trait data classifies as lower relevance while temporal coverage is moderate, yielding an overall low (L) relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Primary data are individual and farm-level laying dates (phenological traits) with nest-box occupancy (%) included (traits + abundance -> high-tier data type), the record spans about 10 years (moderate temporal extent) but spatial extent in km2 is not reported (unknown). By the rule for a high-tier data type combined with unknown spatial extent, overall relevance is reduced to Low.

Primary data are species cover measurements (abundance) from 784 plots across 53 planted and 14 natural riparian sites; site ages span 3–17 years (a temporal gradient), but no spatial extent (km2) or long-term time-series is reported. Data type scores high but lack of reported spatial extent leads to a conservative overall relevance of L for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

Primary data are GPS telemetry occurrence records (presence-only) collected as time-series from 2005–2009, so data-type relevance is low but temporal coverage is moderate; spatial extent is not reported. The dataset is located in Québec (Côte-Nord), triggering a north/south bias modifier and upgrading the overall relevance to moderate for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The authors produced primary genetic datasets (microsatellite and mtDNA) so the data type scores highly for monitoring relevance; however the abstract does not state temporal extent or a quantified spatial extent, so using the decision rules the overall relevance is downgraded to low.

Primary data are GPS telemetry locations (presence-only movement records) collected 2004–2010 on 49 female woodland caribou in Québec (7-year temporal extent = moderate). Presence-only data alone would give low relevance, but the focal species is explicitly described as threatened, so relevance is upgraded to moderate (M).

The primary dataset is a set of habitat-quality raster maps (24 TIFFs) — a spatially explicit distribution-type dataset for an endangered caribou population in Québec. Temporal and spatial extents (years, km2) are not reported in the text, so base relevance is low; it was upgraded one level because the data concern a threatened (endangered) species.

The authors provide primary genotype data (microsatellite loci) and capture locations for caribou in Quebec and Labrador (genetic_analysis = high). However, the abstract/metadata do not specify the temporal duration in years or the spatial extent in km², so temporal and spatial evidence is not sufficient to support high relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring; therefore overall relevance is low.

The authors collected primary plot-based regeneration counts (stems ha⁻¹) — an abundance dataset (high-value data type) — but it represents a single post-harvest survey five years after logging (limited temporal extent) and the study's spatial extent in km² is not reported. Combined, these limitations yield a low relevance score for Quebec biodiversity monitoring despite being multispecies plot data.

Primary data are physiological traits and passage outcomes (presence/absence of passage) for three redhorse species sampled at a single fishway in 2010 and 2012. Temporal extent spans 2010–2012 (moderate) but spatial extent is limited/unstated, and data types map to low/moderate utility; overall conservative relevance for Quebec monitoring is Low.

The study generated primary benthic community abundance and habitat-parameter data (abundance + ecosystem structure) and calculated diversity (species richness), but the abstract gives no temporal or spatial extent. By the relevance rules, abundance data rates high but missing temporal/spatial information reduces overall relevance to L.

The authors generated an experimental dataset measuring HFS abundance and community primary productivity in situ in the St. Lawrence estuary, so data type is highly relevant (abundance + ecosystem function). However, the study is short-term (16 weeks) with no spatial extent reported, so temporal and spatial coverage are limited, yielding a low overall relevance for long-term Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The study generated a primary genomic dataset (GBS: 18,112 SNPs genotyped in 650 individuals from 23 sampling locations) which is highly relevant as genetic analysis for Quebec biodiversity monitoring, but temporal extent and spatial area (km2) are not reported, lowering the score. Sampling in Nunavik (northern Quebec) provides an upgrade, yielding an overall moderate relevance.

The study provides primary abundance (intensity/counts), prevalence (presence-absence) and COI-based genetic data for 16 host species including sites in southwestern Quebec, which yields high-value data types and multispecies coverage. However, temporal duration and explicit spatial extent (km2) are not reported, reducing applicability for long-term or large-scale Quebec monitoring, resulting in a moderate relevance score.

The authors report a primary 2-year systematic survey with quantitative mite counts (abundance data). Temporal coverage is short (1999–2000) and spatial extent is unspecified, limiting applicability, but relevance is increased because two species were recorded for the first time in Quebec.

The study reports primary abundance data (pitfall trap counts) which rates highly for relevance, but temporal extent and spatial area are not specified. Per the relevance rules, a high data type with unknown temporal or spatial extent is downgraded, yielding an overall low relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The study models and maps spatial variations in bird species abundance (data type = abundance) but does not state temporal or spatial extent, lowering confidence. Mention of James Bay and Ungava Bay (northern Quebec) triggers a north/south geographic modifier, upgrading the relevance to moderate for Quebec biodiversity monitoring.

The authors collected abundance data (3,176 specimens) across 73 species over three consecutive years (1997–1999) at two vineyard sites (data type = high relevance). Temporal coverage is moderate (3 years) and spatial extent is not stated, lowering spatial relevance; presence of many species and first records for Quebec increases utility. Overall relevance for Quebec biodiversity monitoring = M (moderate).
